January Bhalukpong is safari country. Pakhui Tiger Reserve is in full swing — elephants at the river, hornbills in the canopy, and the slim but real chance of a tiger sighting.
January clears Champhai's skies to the Myanmar border — the rice terraces lie fallow and the views stretch to another country.
January gives Lunglei its sharpest winter — southern Mizoram's second city under clear, cold skies.
January Mawphlang's sacred forest is a cathedral of moss and silence.
January chills Mokokchung into sharp focus — the Ao Naga heartland under winter-blue skies.
January opens Mon district to outsiders — the last tattooed headhunters of India sit in their doorways, waiting.
January is Moreh at its most comfortable — the border heat retreats and the market energy stays.
January is Namdapha at its most rewarding for wildlife.
January stills Rudrasagar Lake into a mirror — Neermahal palace floats on its own reflection.
January Pasighat is the Siang river at its calmest — clear, blue-green, and framed by hills that catch winter light.
January gives Sivasagar's Ahom monuments the light they deserve — low sun, no haze, no crowds.
January opens Tamenglong's trails without the mud — the wildest district in Manipur at its most navigable.
January Tura is the Garo Hills at their most approachable — 25-degree days, clear skies, and Nokrek Biosphere Reserve to yourself.
January lights Unakoti's rock carvings like a gallery — low sun on 7th-century faces carved into hillside.

February keeps Bhalukpong's safari window wide open.

February keeps the chill but adds longer days — more trail time before dark.
February is Jorhat thawing — the fog retreats and the tea gardens turn photogenic.
February holds Lunglei in dry stillness — the southern Mizo hills at their most walkable.
February Mawphlang is the sacred forest waking up.
February holds Mokokchung in dry stillness — the Ao villages feel like they've always been here, unchanging.
February keeps Mon dry and its mountain roads passable — both rare enough to celebrate.
February keeps Neermahal's lake flat and its birds present — migratory species join the palace as co-attractions.
February warms Pasighat gently. The Siang maintains its winter clarity, tribal villages around town are accessible, and the hills show the first hints of green returning.
February keeps Tamenglong dry and its secrets accessible — if you can handle the basics.
February Tura stays pleasant and empty. The Garo Hills are the least-visited corner of Meghalaya and February keeps it that way.

March wakes Nainital up. Daytime temperatures climb to 15-18°C, rhododendrons start blooming red across the hillsides above Cheena Peak, and the lake turns a proper emerald green.
March pushes Namdapha toward its floral peak.
March warms Neermahal into spring — the lake palace gets colour from Holi celebrations on its shores.
March Nongriat is the last comfortable dry month.
Shivaratri at the Hari+Shankar temple draws devoted…
March brings forest wildflowers and comfortable mornings.
March brings spring energy to Pasighat. Orange orchards in surrounding villages are at their last, the Siang invites kayakers, and the hills explode green.

March Shnongpdeng adds heat to clarity. The river is still transparent and now warm enough for long swims without the January chill.

March brings Rongali Bihu energy to Sivasagar — the Ahom heartland celebrates spring like it invented it.

March warms Tamenglong into its most colourful chapter — oranges ripen and wildflowers compete.

March Tsomgo is a split personality — half the lake frozen, half liquid, with the line moving daily.
March Tura warms up but the Garo Hills compensate with waterfalls waking from dry season sleep.
March Ujjain during Holi is extraordinary — one of the oldest Holi celebrations in India.
March greens Unakoti's forest canopy — the rock carvings gain a living backdrop.

March cracks Ziro wide open. Rhododendrons fire up across the hillsides, the valley greens overnight, and the Apatani rice terraces begin their annual cycle.
April cracks Anini open. The road from Roing becomes passable, the Dibang Valley reveals its scale — enormous, forested, and barely touched.
April Rama Navami at Bhadrachalam is one of India grandest temple…
April cracks Gurudongmar open. The lake sits half-frozen at 5425m — turquoise water meeting white ice under a sky so blue it hurts.
April is peak bloom. Every nursery in Kalimpong is a riot of colour.
April Mahavir Jayanti at the jade temple is a Jain pilgrimage…
April transforms Lolab into the meadow valley it's famous for.
April is Manas before the curtain falls — the park stays open but the heat starts winning.
April is Mokokchung at its most inviting — warm enough to walk, cool enough to enjoy it.
April paints Pfutsero's highlands red — rhododendrons at 2000+ metres, blooming like they're showing off.
April Tsomgo wakes up green. The lake reflects blue sky instead of grey and the surrounding slopes light up with wildflowers.
May warms Almora to a comfortable 20-27°C while the plains below hit 45°C.
May is Anini's most forgiving month. The valley is green and accessible, Idu Mishmi villages are active with spring tasks, and the birding — including potential Mishmi Wren-Babbler sightings —…
May stretches Bomdila's good season one more month.
May holds Dirang's warmth and green without April's crowd pressure.
May is Kalimpong's last hurrah before monsoon.
May Lachen is a staging ground buzzing with jeeps headed to 5425m.
May brings the heat refugees from Delhi, and Mussoorie below gets properly crowded.
May is Mechuka at its most welcoming. The valley is deeply green, trails to surrounding villages are in prime condition, and the Siyom river invites.
May 1st transforms Mokokchung — Moatsu festival turns every Ao village into an open-air celebration.
May is Pfutsero in full bloom — the highlands wear every colour the monsoon will later wash away.
May Tsomgo is the postcard version — deep blue water, green slopes, snow peaks behind.
May holds the tail end of Ziro's golden window.
June gives you Gurudongmar with a ticking clock — the monsoon is coming and every clear morning feels borrowed.
June Lachen catches the last reliable weather before monsoon shuts the curtain.
Sital Sasthi chariot festival — Sambalpur's Rath Yatra equivalent with Shiva-Parvati…
July is Bangus at full bloom. The 30 sq km meadow is carpeted with wildflowers — buttercup, iris, gentian, primula — and the Gujjar shepherd camps dot the valley with woodsmoke and livestock.
August continues peak season with an added wildcard: Ladakh gets occasional cloudbursts now that climate change has introduced monsoon-fringe weather.
August extends the bloom but adds monsoon unpredictability.
August continues Drass's summer at 10-23°C.
August is Lamayuru's busiest month, riding the coattails of Ladakh's overall peak season.
August is Likir's most visited month, riding Ladakh's overall peak season.

September peels the monsoon back. By mid-month, blue sky appears between clouds, the Himalayas flicker in and out of view, and the forests are still saturated green.
September freshness makes the riverside walk pleasant…
September calm returns to the Saraswathi…
September reopens the full 1.5km route — fresh air and dry…
September marks the monsoon's retreat from Bhimtal.
September buzzes with Durga Puja preparation — the city…

September is monsoon's slow retreat from Mussoorie.
September is monsoon's messy exit. The first two weeks still get 100-150 mm of rain with occasional heavy bursts.

September — artisans create Durga Puja decorations and festival…
September reveals the full dam against green…

October opens the dual Baripada experience — Chhau dance + Simlipal…
October reopens Bhalukpong for business.
October returns Champhai's signature view — the Myanmar hills emerge from monsoon like they were always there, waiting.
October trekking through green Eastern Ghats to Odisha highest…
October opens Jorhat back up — ferries run, estates welcome, and Majuli hosts Raas Leela.
October tribal Dussehra celebrations are unique — forest deity…
October paints Lolab in autumn colours that rival New England but with no one watching.
October peels the clouds off Mon's mountains like a slow reveal — and the Konyak villages emerge, vivid.
October reopens Namdapha properly. Animals begin concentrating near water again, leeches retreat, trails firm up, and the forest settles into its spectacular dry-season character.
October returns Neermahal to postcard mode — the lake settles, the boats launch, the palace reflects.
October unveils Pfutsero like it's been saving the view all year — and at 2133 metres, it has.
October clears Phawngpui's stage — the Blue Mountain earns its name again under post-monsoon sky.
October Shnongpdeng comes back to life. The Umngot is clearing day by day and by mid-month the glass-bottom effect returns.
October is Sivasagar reborn — post-monsoon air so clean the Ahom monuments look freshly carved.
October reopens Ukhrul properly — clean air, harvest light, and Tangkhul villages in celebration mode.
October reveals Unakoti freshly washed — four months of rain have cleaned carvings that conservators can't touch.
October Yuksom is the comeback month. The monsoon lifts like a curtain and suddenly Kanchenjunga is right there — massive and impossibly close.
November sharpens everything. The air is crystal clear, the Himalayan wall stands in high definition, and Almora's slate-roofed Kumaoni houses look like a painting against blue sky.
November keeps Anini's clarity but adds urgency — the road window is narrowing.
November cools Bhimtal down to 8-15°C and thins the tourist flow to a trickle.
November keeps October's clarity but sheds its crowds.
November sharpens Champhai to its clearest — every terrace, every ridge, every Myanmar hill defined.
November Dirang trades October's crowds for a quieter kind of beauty.
November sees the first arrivals — the greatest wildlife show on Earth is…
November is Haflong at its most honest — no drama, just clean air and quiet hills.
November is Kalimpong at its most tranquil.
November Lachen is the photographer's month.
November brings the first real cold to Landour, with temperatures between 2-12°C.
November clears Lunglei into its finest version — cool, dry, and entirely yours.
November keeps Mechuka's clarity but drops the temperature.
November is Mokokchung's finest month — the air bites, the views stretch forever, the villages celebrate harvest.
November is Mon at its most welcoming — dry paths, clear air, and Konyak elders with time to spare.
November is Mussoorie powering down for winter.
November is Nainital going to sleep. Temperatures drop sharply: 8-12°C days, 2-4°C nights by month-end.
November gives Neermahal everything — cool air, still water, returning birds, and a palace that knows how to pose.
November sharpens Pfutsero into its clearest self — every ridge, every village, every peak defined.
November gives Phawngpui its clearest summit — the air is cold, the view is infinite, the crowds are zero.
November is peak traffic through the Chicken Neck.
November settles Sivasagar into its best self — cool, clear, and completely yours.
November Tsomgo gets its first ice crystals at the edges while the center stays liquid.
November chills Ukhrul into its sharpest season — every ridge cuts against sky, every trail crunches dry.
November is Unakoti's finest hour — cool air, dry trails, and 10 million rock carvings in perfect condition.
November is Yuksom's sharpest month — the air is thin and clear and Kanchenjunga looks close enough to touch.
November is October's quieter sibling. The valley holds its autumn color a bit longer, crowds thin out, and you get the clarity without the competition.
December is for the tiger reserve and tribal culture — not the…
December freezes Champhai into Christmas mode — Mizo hymns and border-town winter under star-sharp skies.
December drops Haflong to single digits at dawn — bring layers or regret it.
December puts Jorhat's tea gardens to sleep — but the landscape doesn't stop being beautiful.
December Mawphlang wraps in morning mist that burns off by 10am to reveal the sacred forest at its most solemn and still.
December in Mokokchung is Naga Christmas — hymns in Ao dialect echoing through frost-tipped villages.
December freezes Mon into postcard clarity — the mountains sharp, the sky hard blue, the villages wood-smoke warm.
December cools Namdapha into crisp wildlife season.
December fogs Neermahal into mystery — the palace appears through morning mist like a Tripuri painting.
December cools Pasighat into winter mode — mornings are crisp, the Siang is glass-clear, and birding along the riverbanks peaks.
December Siliguri is holiday season in the corridor.
December fogs Sivasagar into something from a period film — Ahom ruins appearing through mist.
December Tura exhales after Wangala. The town is quiet, the weather is perfect, and the Garo Hills are yours without the drums.
December angles winter light across Unakoti's carved faces — shadows reveal details invisible in summer.
